tagline_green: Conditions are working. Go pull on something.
tagline_yellow: Climbable, but compromised — bring patience and a brush.
tagline_red: Stay off the rock for now.
score_blend_overridden: Air & friction alone would have rated:
score_override_raining: It's raining — stay off to protect the rock.
score_override_wet: Still wet inside — let it dry before you climb.
score_override_rule_raining: Climbing wet rock breaks holds and scars the crag — please stay off and protect it.
score_override_rule_wet: While the core is still wet, fragile rock snaps holds and takes damage — please let it dry.
crag_blurb: Compact, fast-draining Ruhrsandstein — dries quicker than soft sandstone, but still never climb it wet.
forecast_note: Best light expected during daylight ({start}:00–{end}:00), with the window when conditions are on.
outlook_window_good: then good until ~{label}, when rain returns
outlook_window_brief: only a brief window — rain back {label}
outlook_no_rain: No rain in the 7-day forecast — go climbing
reason_wet_inside: Rock still wet inside — climbing now breaks holds and damages the crag
reason_wet_inside_wait: Internal moisture lingers after the surface looks dry — wait for it to clear
reason_air_humid: Humid ({rh}% RH) — holds may feel greasy
footer_disclaimer: A hint, not a safety guarantee — always check the rock yourself.
method_lead: Every hour we grab the forecast for {name}, figure out how wet the rock is, and rate conditions from 0–100. That score is the light. The numbers are set for {rock}, the rock you actually climb, and the drying runs on real physics instead of a flat “wait a day after rain” rule. Take it as a hint, not a promise.
method_rules_intro: Two things force a 🔴 by themselves, no matter how good the air is:
method_rule_rain_rest: now, or due within the hour. The rock’s wet.
method_rule_wet_strong: The rock is still wet inside.
method_rule_wet_rest: On fragile rock like {rock}, climbing while the core is still wet snaps holds and scars the crag, even after the surface looks dry.
method_blend_intro: Otherwise the score comes from four things, each weighted by how much it matters for friction on this rock:
method_thresholds_note: Green drops to yellow if the surface is still damp or rain’s due within {h}h. You can still climb, it just won’t be great.
method_s2note: These numbers are tuned to this rock, and other stone behaves differently: granite dries fast and is fine to climb wet, limestone seeps, and grit and sandstone are fragile.
method_s3intro: Rain doesn’t just “clear after a day”. We track two kinds of water in the rock, hour by hour:
method_surface_body: Water sitting on top. It’s what makes holds slick, and it dries fast.
method_core_body: Water soaked into the pores. It dries slowly, so {rock} stays risky for a day or more even once the surface looks dry.
method_et0: How fast they dry comes down to how hard the air is pulling moisture away. Forecasters measure that as reference evapotranspiration (ET₀), worked out from sun, temperature, wind and humidity. So a warm, breezy day dries the rock far quicker than a cold, still one, and near freezing it pretty much stops.
method_caveat_body: ET₀ is really defined for grass, not rock (that’s the FAO-56 standard), so we lean on it only as a stand-in for how fast things dry. The rock-specific bits (how much rain soaks in, how slowly the core dries, the “never climb it wet” rule) come from porous-rock physics and climbing ethics, not from ET₀.
method_eta_explain: The “🪨 likely dry around …” time isn’t a fixed countdown. It’s the first hour the rock should be good to climb again. And if more rain’s on the way, we flag when that window closes.
method_s4post: covers the past 3 days, so we already know how wet the rock is, and the next 7: temperature, humidity, wind, rain and its chance, and ET₀.
method_limit_1: It’s only a model. Nothing here is measured on the actual rock.
method_limit_2: Forecasts are sometimes wrong, and a single crag varies a lot: shade, seepage, the way the rock faces.
method_limit_3: We only look back 3 days, so a wet spell before that is invisible to us.
method_limit_4: Always check the rock yourself. If it’s damp, don’t climb.
method_src_et0: ET₀ definition (a grass reference, used here only as a proxy)
method_src_sandstone: Wet sandstone is weaker (up to ~45% strength loss when saturated, most within hours)
method_src_ethic_tail: : don't climb grit & sandstone when wet.
